answer: Norwegian sentence structure largely follows Subject-Verb-Object (SVO), similar to English. Verbs like 'stanser' (stops) and 'har' (have/has) appear consistent in form across different subjects. Indefinite articles differentiate nouns by class: 'en' for Common and Feminine nouns (e.g., 'en bil', 'en kvinne'), and 'et' for Neuter nouns (e.g., 'et eple'). Definite articles are suffixed to the noun: '-en' for singular Common nouns ('Bilen'), '-a' for singular Feminine nouns ('Kvinna'), and '-et' for singular Neuter nouns ('eplet'). For plural definite nouns, all observed classes use the suffix '-ene' (e.g., 'bilene', 'Kvinnene', 'eplene'), though some nouns may have an irregular stem in the plural ('mann' -> 'Mennene'). Indefinite plural nouns typically add '-er' for Common nouns ('biler') and some Neuter nouns ('epler'). Negation is achieved by placing 'ikke' (not) directly after the verb (e.g., 'har ikke').
